description: Safe food preservation: pressure vs water-bath canning, botulism prevention, fermentation, dehydrating, root cellaring, and freeze failure triage. Specific temperatures, times, and pH thresholds — genuinely dangerous to get wrong.
source: USDA Complete Guide to Home Canning (2015 rev.), National Center for Home Food Preservation, FDA, CDC botulism surveillance data, Ball Blue Book (2020)

triples.subject: home canning
triples.object: use only USDA-tested recipes with exact ingredients and proportions — improvised recipes cannot be verified safe; botulism has no taste, smell, or appearance

triples.subject: botulism
triples.object: Clostridium botulinum spores survive boiling (212°F / 100°C) and produce toxin in low-oxygen, low-acid, moist environments — exactly what a sealed jar provides

triples.subject: botulism
triples.object: toxin is destroyed by boiling 10 minutes; spores require 240–250°F (115–121°C) — achievable only in a pressure canner, not a boiling water bath

triples.subject: pressure canning
triples.object: all low-acid foods: all vegetables (green beans, corn, beets, carrots, potatoes), all meats, poultry, fish, and all mixed products containing them — pH above 4.6

triples.subject: pressure canning
triples.object: 10 PSI at sea level for most foods; at altitudes above 1,000 feet increase to 15 PSI with a weighted gauge, or adjust dial-gauge per USDA table — lower pressure means lower temperature, inadequate kill

triples.subject: pressure canning
triples.object: 240°F (115°C) at 10 PSI — this is the temperature that kills C. botulinum spores over the USDA-tested processing time

triples.subject: water bath canning
triples.object: high-acid foods only: most fruits, properly acidified tomatoes, jams, jellies, pickles with verified acid levels — pH at or below 4.6

triples.subject: water bath canning
triples.object: 212°F (100°C) — kills mold and yeast but NOT C. botulinum spores; safe only when pH below 4.6 prevents spore germination

triples.subject: tomatoes
triples.object: modern tomato varieties may have pH above 4.6 — always add 2 tablespoons bottled lemon juice or 1/2 teaspoon citric acid per quart before water-bath canning; bottled lemon juice has consistent acidity, fresh does not

triples.subject: pH threshold
triples.object: 4.6 pH is the safety boundary — C. botulinum cannot germinate below 4.6; foods above 4.6 require pressure canning regardless of apparent safety

triples.subject: pickle safety
triples.object: use only 5% acidity vinegar (labeled on bottle); do not dilute beyond tested recipe ratios; do not substitute lower-acidity wine vinegar or homemade vinegar

triples.subject: jar seals
triples.object: press center of lid after cooling — should not flex up and down; tap with spoon, should ring clear not dull; any unsealed jar must be refrigerated and used within a few days or reprocessed within 24 hours

triples.subject: canned food
triples.object: discard without tasting: spurting liquid when opened, off odor, mold, damaged or bulging lid — boil home-canned low-acid foods 10 minutes before tasting as an additional safety step

triples.subject: altitude adjustment
triples.object: above 1,000 feet add 5 minutes processing time per 1,000-foot increment (above 3,000 feet add 10 min, 6,000 ft add 15 min) — water boils at lower temperature at altitude

triples.subject: fermentation safety
triples.object: lacto-fermentation acidifies food through lactic acid bacteria — the acid environment (pH drops to 3.0–3.5 in mature ferments) prevents pathogen growth including C. botulinum

triples.subject: fermentation safety
triples.object: use 2–3% salt brine by weight (20–30g salt per liter of water) for vegetables; inadequate salt allows harmful bacteria to outcompete beneficial lactobacillus before acidification occurs

triples.subject: fermentation safety
triples.object: keep vegetables submerged below brine at all times — exposed vegetables can develop mold; white film (kahm yeast) on surface is harmless but remove it; fuzzy colored mold means discard

triples.subject: fermentation
triples.object: 65–75°F (18–24°C) is optimal; below 60°F fermentation stalls; above 80°F ferments too fast and can develop off-flavors or allow unwanted bacteria — refrigerate when target sourness is reached

triples.subject: dehydrating
triples.object: 135°F (57°C) for fruits and vegetables; 160°F (71°C) for meats and jerky (food safety), or pre-heat meat to 160°F in oven before dehydrating if dehydrator cannot reach 160°F

triples.subject: dehydrating
triples.object: properly dried food should be leathery with no visible moisture and should not clump when a handful is squeezed; fruits should be pliable not sticky; vegetables should be brittle

triples.subject: dehydrating
triples.object: store in airtight containers away from light; label with date; vegetables 1 year, fruits 1 year, meats 1–2 months at room temperature or 6 months refrigerated

triples.subject: root cellaring
triples.object: optimal: 32–40°F (0–4°C) with 90–95% humidity for most root vegetables and apples; carrots, beets, and turnips store 4–6 months under these conditions

triples.subject: root cellaring
triples.object: apples emit ethylene gas that accelerates ripening — store separately from other vegetables or in a different area of the cellar

triples.subject: root cellaring
triples.object: onions and garlic prefer cool-dry (60–70°F, low humidity), not cold-moist — they rot in typical root cellar conditions; hang in mesh bags in a cool pantry instead

triples.subject: freezer failure triage
triples.object: a full freezer holds safe temperature for 48 hours without power; half-full for 24 hours — do not open the freezer door; check with thermometer only if needed

triples.subject: freezer failure triage
triples.object: food with ice crystals throughout or temperature at or below 40°F is safe to refreeze, though quality degrades; raw proteins may be cooked and refrozen

triples.subject: freezer failure triage
triples.object: discard any food that has been above 40°F for more than 2 hours — when in doubt, throw it out; the cost of spoiled food is less than the cost of foodborne illness

triples.subject: canning equipment
triples.object: test pressure gauge accuracy yearly at your local cooperative extension office (free service in most states); a faulty gauge is the most common cause of under-processing

triples.subject: canning equipment
triples.object: flat lids (the part with the sealing compound) are single-use only; bands can be reused if not rusted; jars can be reused if free of cracks and chips

triples.subject: USDA tested recipes
triples.object: National Center for Home Food Preservation (nchfp.uga.edu) — free, authoritative, searchable by food type; also available as USDA Complete Guide to Home Canning downloadable PDF
